turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: In case 15-577, Trinity Lutheran Church of Columbia versus Comer, I have the announcement of the judgment and the opinion of the Court except with respect to footnote 3. The Trinity Lutheran Church Child Learning Center is a pre-school and day-care center open throughout the year to serve working families in Boone County, Missouri. It was originally established as a non-profit organization in 1980 before merging with Trinity Lutheran Church in 1985. The center, which operates on church premises, admits students of all religions and enrolment stands at about 90 children ranging from age 2 to 5. At the heart of this case, is the center’s playground. It has your basic play equipment, slides, swings, jungle gyms, monkey bars, and sandboxes. Beneath that play equipment is coarse pea gravel. Youngsters of course often fall on the playground or tumble from the equipment. And when they do, that gravel can be unforgiving. In 2012, the center tried to replace the gravel with a rubber surface by participating in Missouri’s Scrap Tire Program. The program is run by the State’s Department of Natural Resources. It offers reimbursement grants to qualifying non-profit organizations that install playground surfaces made from recycled tires. The department decides who receives the grant based on criteria such as the poverty level in the surrounding area and the applicant’s plan to promote recycling. When the center applied in 2012, the Department had a strict and express policy of denying grants to any applicant owned or controlled by a church. That policy, in the Department’s view, was compelled by Article 1 Section 7 of the Missouri Constitution which says that no money shall be taken from the public treasury in aid of any church. In its application, the sender identified itself as a ministry of Trinity Lutheran Church and then listed the anticipated benefits of its resurfacing project; increasing access to the playground for all children, including those with disabilities; providing a safe, long lasting and resilient surface under the play areas, and improving Missouri’s environment by putting recycled tires to good use. The center scored well, fifth out of the 44 applicants in the 2012 Scrap Tire Program which ultimately handed out 14 grants that year. But despite its high ranking, the center did not receive a grant. It was deemed categorically ineligible because it was operated by Trinity Lutheran Church. Trinity Lutheran sued the department, arguing that its policy of denying the church a grant simply because it was a church violated the Free Exercise Clause of the First Amendment. The district court disagreed, and so did the Court of Appeals for the Eight Circuit over the dissent of one judge. Consistent with a long line of this Court’s cases, we reverse the Court of Appeals for the Eight Circuit and hold that the department’s policy violated the rights of Trinity Lutheran to the free exercise of religion. 70 years ago, this Court said that a state could not exclude someone “from receiving the benefits of public welfare legislation because of their faith or lack of it.” We confirmed their principle three decades later when a plurality of the Court said that, “to condition the availability of benefits upon a recipient’s willingness to surrender his religiously impelled status effectively penalizes the free exercise of his constitutional rights.” The other justices forming a majority in that case agreed. There is much more along the same lines in other cases. The government may not “penalize religious activity by denying any person an equal share of the rights, benefits, and privileges enjoyed by other citizens.” Again, another case, “The government may not impose special disabilities on the basis of religious views or religious status.” But that is precisely what the department’s policy does here. It excludes Trinity Lutheran from participating in the Scrap Tire Program for no reason other than its being a church. Now, it is true that the Department’s policy does not prohibit the church from engaging in any religious conduct or holding any religious belief. But what we said 50 years ago remains true. “It is too late in the day to doubt that the liberties of religion may be infringed by the denial of or placing of conditions upon a benefit or privilege.” And as for the Department’s argument that Trinity Lutheran’s free exercised rights could not have been violated because it was never entitled to a subsidy in the first place, Trinity Lutheran is not claiming any entitlement to a subsidy. It is asking only to participate equally in a government benefit program without having to disavow its religious character. Trinity Lutheran is a member of the community too and should not have to give up its identity to participate in community benefits. Given what our cases say, and given what the Missouri Policy does, the conclusion that the policy violates Trinity Lutheran’s free exercised rights follows inexorably and is unremarkable. Nearly 200 years ago, a legislator urged the Maryland Assembly to adopt a bill that would end the state’s disqualification of dues from public office. This is what he said, “If on account of my religious faith I am subjected to disqualifications from which others are free, I cannot consider myself a persecuted man. An odious exclusion from any of the benefits common to the rest of my fellow citizens is a persecution, differing only in degree but of a nature equally unjustifiable with those that whose instruments are chains and torture.” Now, the Missouri Department of Natural Resources has not subjected anyone to chains or torture on account of religion. And the result of the state’s policy is nothing so dramatic as the denial of political office. The consequence is, in all likelihood, a few extra scraped knees. But the exclusion of Trinity Lutheran from a public benefit for which it is otherwise qualified solely because it is a church is odious to our constitution all the same and cannot stand. We accordingly reverse the judgment of the Eight Circuit and remand for further proceedings consistent with this opinion. Justice Thomas and Justice Gorsuch have each filed an opinion concurring in part in which the other has joined. Justice Breyer has filed an opinion concurring in the judgment. And Justice Sotomayor has filed a dissenting opinion which Justice Ginsburg has joined.
